abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
Post-MI complications relevant to CTICU

Post-MI complications include arrhythmias, cardiogenic shock, acute heart failure, papillary muscle rupture with severe mitral regurgitation, ventricular septal rupture, free wall rupture with tamponade, RV infarction, pericarditis, mural thrombus and recurrent ischaemia. CTICU patients may arrive after PCI, CABG, mechanical support or emergency surgery, so the nurse must connect new haemodynamic patterns with mechanical complications.

Cardiogenic shock — Tissue hypoperfusion caused primarily by cardiac pump failure, often with hypotension, low cardiac index, high filling pressures and rising lactate.

Mechanical complication — Structural damage after MI such as papillary muscle rupture, VSD or free wall rupture causing abrupt haemodynamic collapse.

Complication Early signs Immediate focus
Papillary muscle rupture / acute MR Flash pulmonary oedema, new murmur, shock, large V waves if PA/CVP context. Urgent echo, senior/cardiac surgical review, ventilatory and vasoactive support.
Ventricular septal rupture Shock, new harsh systolic murmur, step-up oxygen saturation on right heart cath. Urgent echo and surgical/interventional review.
Free wall rupture Tamponade, PEA, sudden collapse. Arrest response and surgical emergency.
RV infarction Hypotension, raised JVP/CVP, clear lungs, inferior MI pattern. Maintain RV preload carefully, avoid hypoxia/hypercarbia/acidosis, cautious nitrates.

Escalate immediately for post-MI shock, new murmur with pulmonary oedema, sudden PEA, suspected mechanical complication, recurrent ST elevation, sustained VT/VF, or rapidly rising lactate.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Why complications occur

Myocardial infarction injures contractile tissue and conduction pathways. Early complications are often electrical or pump-related: VT/VF, bradyarrhythmia, AV block, acute LV failure and cardiogenic shock. Mechanical complications typically occur when necrotic myocardium loses structural integrity. Modern reperfusion has reduced but not eliminated these events, and when they occur they are high mortality and time-critical.

Papillary muscle rupture, usually involving the posteromedial papillary muscle due to single blood supply, causes acute severe mitral regurgitation. The left atrium has no time to dilate, so pulmonary oedema and shock occur abruptly. Ventricular septal rupture creates a left-to-right shunt, reducing effective systemic output and causing pulmonary overcirculation. Free wall rupture causes haemopericardium and tamponade, often presenting as sudden PEA.

RV infarction is a physiology trap. The LV may be underfilled because the RV cannot deliver blood through the pulmonary circulation. These patients may have hypotension with clear lungs and high CVP/JVP. They are sensitive to preload reduction, hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and tachyarrhythmia. Maintaining sinus rhythm and AV synchrony may be crucial.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls

A new murmur plus shock after MI is a mechanical complication until proven otherwise.

Flash pulmonary oedema after inferior/posterior MI should raise concern for acute severe MR from papillary muscle dysfunction or rupture.

PEA after MI may be free wall rupture with tamponade; do not treat it as generic arrest only.

Nitrates and aggressive diuresis can harm RV infarction patients if they are preload dependent and hypotensive.

Mechanical complications need echo and definitive intervention. Vasoactive drugs are bridges, not cures.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In

Echo and haemodynamic devices

Bedside echo is the key diagnostic tool for suspected mechanical complications. It can identify severe MR, VSD, tamponade, RV dysfunction, LV failure and regional wall motion abnormality. PA catheter data may show high filling pressures, low CI, large V waves in severe MR, pulmonary hypertension or oxygen saturation step-up in shunt physiology, but interpretation is senior-led.

Tool Useful for Nursing role
12-lead ECG Recurrent ischaemia, infarct territory, conduction block. Acquire rapidly and repeat with changes.
Arterial line Shock severity, pulse pressure, response to therapy. Ensure waveform accuracy and trend MAP/pulse pressure.
PA catheter CI, filling pressures, SvO2, MR/VSD clues. Ensure accurate levelling/zeroing and safe sampling if trained.
Ventilator Supports pulmonary oedema and oxygen delivery. Avoid hypoxia/hypercarbia especially in RV failure.
Defibrillator VT/VF and unstable tachyarrhythmia. Pads on early in high-risk patients.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Targets and Numbers

Parameter Target / threshold Interpretation
Cardiogenic shock BP SBP often <90 mmHg or need support to maintain MAP, with hypoperfusion. Do not rely on BP alone; lactate, UO and mentation matter.
Cardiac index Concern <2.2 L/min/m2 with hypoperfusion. Supports cardiogenic shock phenotype.
PAOP Often high in LV failure; may be very high with acute MR. Interpret with echo and clinical picture.
V waves Large V waves may suggest severe MR but are not perfectly specific. Escalate for echo if new pulmonary oedema/shock.
RV infarct clues Inferior MI + hypotension + clear lungs + high CVP/JVP. Avoid unnecessary preload reduction; urgent senior plan.
MAP Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered. Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours. Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support. After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk. Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk. Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: A new systolic murmur with shock after MI should trigger concern for:
options: Mechanical complication such as MR or VSD
quiz.explanation: Mechanical complications after MI can present with new murmur and shock and require urgent echo and senior review.

quiz.question: Which pattern suggests RV infarction?
options: Inferior MI with hypotension, raised CVP/JVP and relatively clear lungs
Anterior MI with severe pulmonary oedema only
quiz.explanation: RV infarction impairs RV output, causing systemic venous congestion and low LV preload, often without pulmonary oedema initially.

quiz.question: Why are vasoactive drugs only a bridge in papillary muscle rupture?
options: The structural valve failure needs definitive surgical/interventional correction
Vasoactive drugs permanently repair papillary muscle
quiz.explanation: Acute severe MR from rupture is a mechanical problem. Drugs support perfusion while definitive intervention is arranged.
